Why the Blitzkrieg failed in Russia

# Why the Blitzkrieg failed in Russia

### Operation Barbarossa

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jun 22, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

*Blitzkrieg- The word, meaning "lightning war", in its strategic means is associated with a series of quick and decisive short battles to deliver a knockout blow to an enemy state before it could fully mobilize- From Wikipedia*

The success of a [Blitzkrieg](https://www.quora.com/Why-didnt-the-Nazi-invasion-to-the-Soviet-Union-succeed/answer/Ratnakar-Sadasyula-%E0%B0%B0%E0%B0%A4%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%A8%E0%B0%BE%E0%B0%95%E0%B0%B0%E0%B1%8D-%E0%B0%B8%E0%B0%A6%E0%B0%B8%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%AF%E0%B1%81%E0%B0%B2) tactic works in countries that are smaller in size, and which are faster to cover. Hitler's early success came in places like France, Belgium, Holland, Poland, most of them countries that could be covered from end to end without taking too much time. So the strategy worked here well, there was hardly any time to recover from the attacks and recoup. It also did not help that most of these nations did not have strong armies either.

The case of Soviet Russia, is different, the country was huge, it spanned two continents, it stretched from the Black Sea to the Urals to Siberia. Add to it, it did not have a uniform terrain either, it was a mix of plains, marshlands, woodlands, mountains. What this essentially meant, that the Soviet leadership had enough time to recoup from the early reverses it suffered, and strike back. Initially the Nazis had a succesful run, overcoming most of the Western part of Soviet Russia, giving Hitler enough confidence to declare for a victory rally in Moscow.

While Stalingrad is rightly considered the turning point during the War, the Nazis had suffered a reverse much earlier during Operation Typhoon, the operation to capture Moscow. Just when the Nazis were within 140 km of the city, the weather deteriorated badly, slowing down their advance, and on Oct 31,1941, the operation was halted. This gave the Soviets enough time to regroup themselves, and also reorganize their armies. The long trek into Russia, also exhausted most of the German soldiers, it was akin to Napoleon's failed invasion of Russia, in 1812. When the Germans again attacked and advanced on Moscow, the dreaded Russian winter had set in store, which they were not at all accustomed to. Nor was the Wehrmacht adept at Winter Warfare, which the Russians were more used to. The failure to capture Moscow hit the Nazis morale, and many of them were not clearly in the fight, physically as well as mentally.  
  
One more factor was the resolve of the Soviets themselves and the Great Patriotic War mood drummed up by the Communist Regime. Not that they had any option, any one fleeing from the battlefield was immediately shot dead as a deserter, those who lost the battle, were executed as traitors. The Soviets had two choices- die on the battle field, or survive it and be executed on charges of treason. But that itself was not the main factor, there was a huge upsurge of patriotic, nationalist sentiment, with regular calls to defend the Fatherland. The Soviet Union also had the capacity to raise large number of men, in no short time, even though they suffered losses that often went into millions. The Red Army had the strength to raise resources, even after losing a whole lot during the early stages. The German Army was also not equipped for a long drawn out combat, it's strength was the Blitzkrieg type, fast and swift attacks, it could not sustain a long campaign.
